input.draft_text:
Section 24 of the Income Tax Act: Deductions on Home Loan Interest
Everyone dreams of buying a home at least once in their lifetime. For most people, this dream is made possible only through a home loan, and it often becomes one of the longest financial commitments you take on. To reduce this burden, the Income Tax Act provides a specific deduction on home loan interest under Sec 24 of the Income Tax.
Section 24 of the Income Tax Act allows you to claim deductions on interest paid on borrowed capital for a house property, subject to defined limits and conditions. Understanding how this section works, especially across self-occupied and let-out house properties and under different tax regimes, is essential for accurate tax planning and helps lower the taxable income lawfully.
What Is Section 24 of the Income Tax Act?
Income Tax Section 24 covers deductions for income from house property. It lets taxpayers claim a standard deduction of 30% on rental income, plus a deduction for interest paid on home loans.
Even if it is a self-occupied house, and you don't earn any income from it. You can still deduct the interest paid on your home loan, up to ₹2 lakh per year.
Income from House Property Under the Income Tax Act
When it comes to taxes, the Income Tax Act says that any money you make from a house or property is taxed under the Income from House Property category. This includes:
The money you get from renting out a place.
Notional or deemed rental income from properties treated as let out for tax purposes
If you own more than two houses, the extra ones are thought to be rented out, even if they aren't. So, the rent you're expected to get from those properties is taxable. If you live in the house yourself, this value can be zero or even negative if you're claiming home loan interest as a deduction.
Section 24 of the Income Tax Act: Deductions on House Property
Under the Income Tax Act, income earned from a house property is taxed under a separate head called Income from House Property. Section 24 specifies the rules for computing this income, and the following deductions can be claimed.
Tax Deduction on Rental Income
Rental income received from a let-out or which is deemed let-out property is eligible for the following deductions.
Municipal Tax
Municipal taxes paid to local authorities can be deducted from the Gross Annual Value of the property to arrive at the Net Annual Value. This deduction is allowed only if the municipal tax is borne by the property owner and actually paid during the relevant financial year. Municipal taxes that are unpaid or borne by the tenant are not allowed as a deduction.
Standard Deduction
Section 24 lets you lower your property's net annual value by 30% after you have paid any municipal taxes. You are eligible for the deduction whether or not you actually spent money on stuff like repairs, upkeep, insurance, or anything else related to the property.
Tax Benefit on Repayment of Home Loan Interest
Section 24 allows a deduction for the interest portion of a home loan taken for the purchase, construction, repair, or renovation of a house property.
For a self-occupied or vacant property, the maximum deduction allowed is ₹2 lakh per financial year, subject to completion timelines and other conditions.
For a let-out property, the entire interest paid on the home loan is allowed as a deduction, with no upper limit, although loss set-off rules apply.
Under the new tax regime, this interest deduction is not available for self-occupied properties.
Pre-Construction Interest
Pre-construction interest refers to interest paid on a home loan during the period before construction is completed or possession is obtained. This interest cannot be claimed as a deduction in the years in which it is paid.
Instead:
The total pre-construction interest is accumulated.
The deduction can be taken in five equal yearly instalments.
The deduction begins from the year in which the construction is completed
For self-occupied properties, the combined deduction of current-year interest and pre-construction interest cannot exceed ₹2 lakh in any financial year.
Conditions to Claim Interest Deduction Under Section 24(b)
Under Section 24(b) of the Income Tax Act, a deduction for home loan interest is allowed, subject to specific conditions, which are as follows.
The loan should be dated on or after April 1, 1999.
You must have used the loan to build or buy a new house.
You have five years from the end of the fiscal year when you got the loan to finish building or buying the place.
Make sure you have an interest certificate that shows how much interest you owe on the loan.
Under Section 24b of the Income Tax Act, you can deduct up to Rs. 2,00,000. This applies to both rented and self-occupied homes. However, under certain circumstances, this limit can drop to Rs. 30,000.
Eligibility Criteria for Claiming Deduction
Any individual who owns a residential property, whether it is self-occupied or rented out, can claim deductions under Section 24. These deductions relate to interest paid on a home loan taken for the property.
You can claim both HRA and home loan interest under certain circumstances, like
The owned house is in one city, and the taxpayer lives on rent in another city.
The house is in the same city, but the taxpayer stays on rent due to work or schooling needs.
The property purchased through a home loan is still under construction.
But the following deductions are only available if you file taxes under the old tax regime.
How to Compute Income from House Property?
Let’s understand the computation of income from house property with a simple and practical example.
Example:
Viraj Verma has taken a home loan and repays ₹4,00,000 annually, out of which ₹2,00,000 is interest. He has also incurred pre-construction interest of ₹3,00,000.
He earns ₹8,000 per month as rent from a property and pays municipal taxes of ₹4,000 annually. Let’s calculate his income from the house property in the two different cases.
The property is self-occupied
The property is let out
Things to Consider When Calculating Income from House Property
When computing income from house property, here are a few things to keep in mind –
The income tax rules might change with time. Keep abreast of the latest changes to compute your income properly.
If you choose the new tax regime, the deduction on home loan interest under Section 24(b) is not available.
The standard deduction is available only on deemed-to-be-let-out and let-out house properties.
If you feel confused, you can take the help of tax professionals to claim tax deductions on income from house property.
Key Takeaways
Income from house property is taxed separately and depends on whether the property is self-occupied, let out, or deemed as let out.
Municipal taxes actually paid by the owner can be deducted before calculating taxable income.
A standard deduction of 30%Fis available only for let-out and deemed let-out house properties, regardless of actual expenses.
Home loan interest can be claimed under Section 24, subject to limits, with higher benefits available for let-out house properties.
Pre-construction interest is allowed only after completion of construction and must be claimed in five equal instalments.
You can set off loss from house property against other income only up to ₹2 lakh per year, with the balance carried forward for up to eight years.
Conclusion
Understanding how income from house property is computed under Section 24 of the Income Tax Act is essential for anyone who owns a home, whether it is self-occupied or rented out. From determining the correct annual value to claiming deductions for municipal taxes, standard deduction, and home loan interest, each step plays a role in arriving at the final taxable figure. Applying these rules correctly can help reduce tax liability, avoid errors during filing, and ensure full compliance with income tax provisions.
While tax deductions ease the financial burden of owning a home, a home loan remains a long-term financial commitment. Hence, homeowners must also plan for risk. Life insurance acts as a financial safeguard, ensuring that outstanding home loan liabilities do not fall on dependents in case of any unfortunate events.
FAQs
Can I Claim Deductions Under Both Section 24 and Section 80EE?
Yes, you can claim deductions under both sections 24 and section 80ee together.
What Is the Maximum Deduction Limit Under Section 24 for a Self-Occupied Property?
For a self-occupied property, the maximum deduction allowed under Section 24 is ₹2 lakh per financial year on the interest paid on a home loan, subject to prescribed conditions. This deduction is not available under the new tax regime.
Can I Claim Both HRA and Home Loan Tax Benefits?
Yes, you can claim both HRA exemption and home loan deduction if the reasons for living on rent are genuine, such as a faraway work location or an under-construction house. This benefit is available only under the old tax regime.
Can Co-Owners of a House Property Claim Tax Deductions Jointly?
Yes, co-owners of a house property can claim tax deductions jointly, provided each co-owner is also a co-borrower in the home loan and contributes to the EMI. The deduction is allowed in proportion to their ownership share in the property.
Is Brokerage or Commission Paid Allowed as a Deduction Under Section 24?
No, brokerage or commission paid is not allowed as a deduction under section 24
Can I Claim Deduction on Interest Paid for Two House Properties?
Tax benefits on a second home loan are allowed, with interest fully deductible for rented properties and restricted to ₹2 lakh in total if both houses are self-occupied.
How Does the New Tax Regime Affect Deductions Under Section 24?
Under the new tax regime, no deduction is allowed under Section 24 for interest on self-occupied properties, and principal repayment deductions are also not available. However, for let-out house properties, interest on home loans can still be claimed without any ceiling, regardless of the tax regime.
Disclaimer

While reasonable care has been taken in compiling the information, Bajaj Life Insurance Limited (Formerly known as Bajaj Allianz Life Insurance Company Limited) assumes no liability for its accuracy. The opinions expressed do not constitute formal recommendations, professional advice, or definitive guidance. Readers are encouraged to conduct their own due diligence and are advised to seek independent professional or expert advice before making any financial or investment decisions based on the content. Any illustrations included are for conceptual clarity only and do not reflect the actual performance of any product or offering.
Tax benefits as per prevailing Income tax laws shall apply. Please check with your tax consultant for eligibility

output.final_text:
Section 24 of the Income Tax Act: Deductions on Home Loan Interest
Everyone dreams of buying a home at least once in their lifetime. For most people, this dream is made possible only through a home loan, and it often becomes one of the longest financial commitments you take on. To reduce this burden, the Income Tax Act provides a specific deduction on home loan interest under Sec 24 of the Income Tax.
Section 24 of the Income Tax Act allows you to claim deductions on interest paid on borrowed capital for a house property, subject to defined limits and conditions. Understanding how this section works, especially across self-occupied and let-out house properties and under different tax regimes, is essential for accurate tax planning and helps lower the taxable income lawfully.
What Is Section 24 of the Income Tax Act?1
Income Tax Section 24 covers deductions for income from house property. It lets taxpayers claim a standard deduction of 30% on rental income, plus a deduction for interest paid on home loans.
Even if it is a self-occupied house, and you don't earn any income from it. You can still deduct the interest paid on your home loan, up to ₹2 lakh per year.
Income from House Property Under the Income Tax Act
When it comes to taxes, the Income Tax Act says that income earned from a house or property is taxed under the Income from House Property category. This includes:
1. The money you get from renting out a place. 2. Annual value of the properties treated as let out for tax purposes
If you own more than two houses, the extra ones are thought to be rented out, even if they aren't. So, the rent you're expected to get from those properties is taxable. If you live in the house yourself, this value can be zero or even negative if you're claiming home loan interest as a deduction.
Section 24 of the Income Tax Act: Deductions on House Property1
Under the Income Tax Act, income earned from a house property is taxed under a separate head called Income from House Property. Section 24 specifies the rules for computing this income, and the following deductions can be claimed.
1. Tax Deduction on Rental Income
Rental income received from a let-out or which is deemed let-out property is eligible for the following deductions.
● Municipal Tax
Municipal taxes paid to local authorities can be deducted from the Gross Annual Value of the property to arrive at the Net Annual Value only if the owner bears the municipal tax and has paid the tax during that financial year.
● Standard Deduction
Section 24 lets you lower your property's net annual value by 30% after you have paid any municipal taxes. You are eligible for the deduction whether or not you have actually incurred expenditure on stuff like repairs, upkeep, insurance, or anything else related to the property.
2. Tax Benefit on Repayment of Home Loan Interest
Section 24 allows a deduction for the interest portion of a home loan taken for the purchase, construction, repair, or renovation of a house property.
● For a self-occupied or vacant property, the maximum deduction allowed is ₹2 lakh per financial year, subject to completion timelines and other conditions.
● For a let-out property, the entire interest paid on the home loan is allowed as a deduction.
● The interest deduction is allowed only up to ₹30,000 if you take a home loan for repairs or renovations of the home or if the construction or purchase of the home has not been completed within 5 years from the end of the financial year when you took the loan.
Under the new tax regime, this interest deduction is not available for self-occupied properties.
3. Pre-Construction Interest
Pre-construction interest refers to interest paid on a home loan during the period before construction is completed. This interest payable during the construction phase cannot be claimed as a deduction in the years when you are paying it.
Instead:
● The total pre-construction interest is accumulated.
● The deduction can be taken in five equal yearly instalments.
● The deduction begins from the year in which the construction is completed
For self-occupied properties, the combined deduction of current-year interest on a housing loan and pre-construction interest cannot exceed ₹2 lakh in any financial year.
Conditions to Claim Interest Deduction Under Section 24(b)
Under Section 24(b) of the Income Tax Act, a deduction for home loan interest is allowed, subject to specific conditions, which are as follows2.
1. The loan should be dated on or after April 1, 1999. 2. You must have used the loan to build or buy a new house. 3. You have five years from the end of the fiscal year from when you get the loan to finish
building or getting acquisition of the place. 4. Make sure you have an interest certificate that shows how much interest you owe on the
loan. Under Section 24b of the Income Tax Act, you can deduct up to Rs. 2,00,000. This applies to both rented and self-occupied homes. However, under certain circumstances, this limit can drop to Rs. 30,000.
Eligibility Criteria for Claiming Deduction for Home Loan and HRA
Any individual who owns a residential property, whether it is self-occupied or rented out, can claim deductions under Section 24. These deductions relate to interest paid on a home loan taken for the property.
You can claim both HRA and home loan interest under certain circumstances, like3
1. The house on which the home loan is taken is in one city, and the taxpayer lives on rent in
another city. 2. The house on which the home loan is taken is in the same city, but the taxpayer stays on
rent due to work or schooling needs. 3. The property purchased through a home loan is still under construction and you live on rent
elsewhere. 4. You let out the home that you bought with a home loan and you live on rent elsewhere.
But the following deductions are only available if you file taxes under the old tax regime.
How to Compute Income from House Property?
Let’s understand the computation of income from house property with a simple and practical example.
Example:
Viraj Verma has taken a home loan and repays ₹4,00,000 annually, out of which ₹2,00,000 is interest. He has also incurred pre-construction interest of ₹3,00,000.
He earns ₹8,000 per month as rent from a property and pays municipal taxes of ₹4,000 annually. Let’s calculate his income from the house property in the two different cases.
The property is self-occupied
1. The property is let out
Particulars Self-Occupied Property Let-Out Property
Gross Annual Value (Rent – ₹8,000 × 12) NIL 96,000
Less: Municipal Taxes NA 4,000
Net Annual Value (NAV) NIL 92,000
Less: Standard Deduction (30% of NAV) NA 27,600
Less: Interest on Home Loan 2,00,000 2,00,000
Less: Pre-construction Interest (1/5th of ₹3,00,000)
60,000 60,000
Total Interest consideration upto limit. 2,00,000 2,60,000
Income from House Property 2,00,000 (1,95,600)
Things to Consider When Calculating Income from House Property
When computing income from house property, here are a few things to keep in mind1 –
1. The income tax rules might change with time. Keep abreast of the latest changes to compute
your income properly. 2. If you choose the new tax regime, the deduction on home loan interest under Section 24(b)
is not available. 3. The standard deduction is available only on deemed-to-be-let-out and let-out house
properties. 4. If you feel confused, you can take the help of tax professionals to claim tax deductions on
income from house property.
Key Takeaways
1. Income from house property is taxed separately and depends on whether the property is
self-occupied, let out, or deemed as let out. 2. Municipal taxes actually paid by the owner can be deducted before calculating taxable
income. 3. A standard deduction of 30%is available only for let-out and deemed let-out house
properties, regardless of actual expenses1. 4. Home loan interest can be claimed under Section 24, subject to limits, with higher benefits
available for let-out house properties. 5. Pre-construction interest is allowed only after completion of construction and must be
claimed in five equal instalments. 6. You can set off loss from house property against other income only up to ₹2 lakh per year,
with the balance carried forward for up to eight years.
Conclusion
Understanding how income from house property is computed under Section 24 of the Income Tax Act is essential for anyone who owns a home, whether it is self-occupied or rented out. From determining the correct annual value to claiming deductions for municipal taxes, standard deduction, and home loan interest, each step plays a role in arriving at the final taxable figure. Applying these rules correctly can help reduce tax liability, avoid errors during filing, and ensure full compliance with income tax provisions.
While tax deductions ease the financial burden of owning a home, a home loan remains a long-term financial commitment. Hence, homeowners must also plan for risk. Life insurance acts as a financial safeguard, ensuring that outstanding home loan liabilities do not fall on dependents in case of any unfortunate events.
FAQs
1. Can I Claim Deductions Under Both Section 24 and Section 80EE?
Yes, you can claim deductions under both sections 24 and section 80ee together1.
2. What Is the Maximum Deduction Limit Under Section 24 for a Self-Occupied Property?
For a self-occupied property, the maximum deduction allowed under Section 24 is ₹2 lakh per financial year on the interest paid on a home loan, subject to prescribed conditions1. This deduction is not available under the new tax regime1.
3. Can I Claim Both HRA and Home Loan Tax Benefits?
Yes, you can claim both HRA exemption and home loan deduction if the reasons for living on rent are genuine, such as a faraway work location or an under-construction house. This benefit is available only under the old tax regime.
4. Can Co-Owners of a House Property Claim Tax Deductions Jointly?
Yes, co-owners of a house property can claim tax deductions jointly, provided each co-owner is also a co-borrower in the home loan and is responsible for paying the EMI. Moreover, the
construction of the home should be completed since the tax benefit is available from the year of completion of construction. The deduction is allowed in proportion to their ownership share in the property4.
5. Is Brokerage or Commission Paid Allowed as a Deduction Under Section 24?
No, brokerage or commission paid is not allowed as a deduction under section 245
6. Can I Claim Deduction on Interest Paid for Two House Properties?
Tax benefits on a second home loan are allowed, with interest fully deductible for rented properties and restricted to ₹2 lakh in total if both houses are self-occupied6.
7. How Does the New Tax Regime Affect Deductions Under Section 24?
Under the new tax regime, no deduction is allowed under Section 24 for interest on self- occupied properties, and principal repayment deductions are also not available. However, for let-out house properties, interest on home loans can still be claimed without any ceiling,.
